stem: A 7-week-old former 38-week infant presents to a metropolitan paediatric emergency department in winter with 4 days of coryza and 24 hours of worsening work of breathing. He has been refusing feeds for 8 hours and has had 3 short apnoeas witnessed in triage. He is on standard nasal-prong oxygen at 1 L/min. RSV PCR is positive. He has had no fever above 38.0 degrees C and chest X-ray shows hyperinflation without focal consolidation. The treating registrar has commenced him on 2 L/kg/min heated humidified high-flow nasal cannula with FiO2 0.4 thirty minutes ago.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 78, SpO2 89% on HFNC FiO2 0.4, temperature 37.6 degrees C, CRT 3 seconds
data_table.Examination: Marked subcostal and intercostal recession, head bobbing, fine crackles bilaterally, mottled peripheries
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.24, pCO2 64 mmHg, HCO3 26, lactate 2.1 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue current HFNC settings and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Persistent hypercapnia, falling saturations and recurrent apnoeas on established HFNC define HFNC failure; further passive observation risks respiratory arrest. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial nebulised adrenaline 5 mg with reassessment at 15 minutes
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline has not been shown to alter the natural history of bronchiolitis and does not address the apnoeas or hypercapnia driving deterioration here. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; PREDICT bronchiolitis guideline)

options.text: Order an urgent CT chest and consult paediatric infectious diseases
options.rationale: Imaging beyond the existing chest X-ray will not change acute management; the priority is ventilatory support, and apnoeas in RSV infants are characteristic and do not need ID review. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer IV salbutamol 5 mcg/kg over 10 minutes and reassess
options.rationale: Beta-2 agonists are not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months; salbutamol risks tachycardia and lactataemia without bronchodilator response in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Escalate to non-invasive ventilation with PEEP 6 cmH2O and contact PICU
options.rationale: HFNC failure (rising pCO2, apnoeas, persistent saturations under 90%) in an infant under 3 months meets escalation criteria; NIV with PEEP recruits collapsed alveoli and PICU transfer secures airway support. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; ANZICS paediatric NIV)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis escalation in Australian paediatric units follows a stepwise model: minimal handling and nasogastric or IV fluids, supplemental oxygen, HFNC for SpO2 under 92 percent or moderate work of breathing, then NIV or intubation for HFNC failure. HFNC failure is defined by persistent SpO2 under 90 percent at FiO2 above 0.4, rising pCO2 above 60 mmHg, or recurrent apnoeas. NIV with PEEP 5 to 7 cmH2O is the next step before intubation in most centres. PICU contact is mandatory at the point of HFNC failure.
key_points: HFNC failure criteria: FiO2 over 0.4 with SpO2 under 90 percent, pCO2 over 60 mmHg, or apnoeas
Salbutamol and adrenaline are not recommended in infants under 12 months with bronchiolitis
Apnoeas are typical of RSV in infants under 3 months and warrant high-acuity observation
Escalate to NIV before intubation when criteria met; involve PICU early
explanation.pearls: In an infant under 3 months on HFNC at 2 L/kg/min with FiO2 above 0.4 and any apnoea within 30 minutes, escalate to NIV that hour - do not retry HFNC settings.

subtopic_detail: Paediatric DKA: fluid plan and cerebral oedema
stem: A 9-year-old girl presents to a regional emergency department with 36 hours of vomiting, polyuria and progressive drowsiness. She is newly diagnosed with type 1 diabetes mellitus. She weighs 28 kg and is judged to be 8 percent dehydrated clinically. The closest paediatric endocrinology service is 4 hours away by road. The intern asks you to prescribe her IV fluid regimen for the first 48 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, BP 96/58, RR 32 (Kussmaul), SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 degrees C, GCS 14
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, capillary refill 3 seconds, peripheries cool, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 28 mmol/L, Na 138 mmol/L (corrected 142), K 4.1, urea 9.1, bicarbonate 6, pH 7.06, ketones 5.4 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial fluid plan?

options.text: Bolus 20 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride then maintenance plus 8% deficit over 24 hours
options.rationale: Replacing the deficit over 24 hours and giving a 20 mL/kg bolus delivers fluid too rapidly in paediatric DKA and is linked to cerebral oedema; the deficit should be spread over 48 hours and boluses limited. (Source: ISPAD 2022; RCH CPG: Diabetic ketoacidosis)

options.text: Bolus 10 mL/kg 4% albumin then maintenance plus 5% deficit over 24 hours
options.rationale: Colloid offers no benefit over crystalloid in paediatric DKA and using a 5% deficit underestimates her clinically assessed 8% dehydration. (Source: ISPAD 2022)

options.text: Bolus 20 mL/kg Hartmann's then 1.5 times maintenance with no deficit calculation
options.rationale: Failing to calculate the deficit underestimates volume needs and the large bolus carries the same cerebral oedema risk; Hartmann's is acceptable but the rate is wrong. (Source: ISPAD 2022)

options.text: Start an insulin infusion at 0.1 unit/kg/hour with no separate fluid prescription
options.rationale: Insulin should not be started before adequate fluid resuscitation and not in the first hour; starting insulin alone fails to address hypoperfusion and risks rapid osmolar shift. (Source: ISPAD 2022; AMH Children's Dosing Companion)

options.text: Bolus 10 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride then maintenance plus 8% deficit over 48 hours
options.rationale: A single 10 mL/kg bolus restores perfusion without precipitating cerebral oedema; the remaining deficit plus maintenance is run evenly over 48 hours per Australian and international paediatric DKA protocols. (Source: ISPAD 2022; RCH CPG: Diabetic ketoacidosis)

explanation.summary: Paediatric DKA management balances rehydration against cerebral oedema risk. Australian and ISPAD protocols favour modest initial bolus volumes (10 mL/kg), spreading remaining deficit over 48 hours rather than 24, delaying insulin by at least an hour after fluids commence, and starting at 0.05 to 0.1 unit/kg/hour. Cerebral oedema risk factors include age under 5 years, new-onset diabetes, severe acidosis (pH under 7.1), low pCO2 and elevated urea. Recognising signs (headache, bradycardia, hypertension, falling GCS) and treating with hypertonic saline 3% 2.5 to 5 mL/kg are essential.
key_points: Bolus 10 mL/kg only - repeat only if perfusion still inadequate
Spread deficit + maintenance over 48 hours, not 24
Start insulin at 0.05 to 0.1 unit/kg/hour, at least 1 hour after fluids
Monitor neurologically every hour for cerebral oedema
Hypertonic saline 3% 2.5 to 5 mL/kg is the first-line treatment for cerebral oedema
explanation.pearls: If pH is under 7.1, age under 5 years, or pCO2 under 18 mmHg, the cerebral oedema risk is roughly doubled - 2-hourly neuro obs are mandatory and hypertonic saline 3% must be drawn up at the bedside.

stem: A 6-year-old boy with known asthma presents to a metropolitan paediatric emergency department with progressive wheeze and respiratory distress following a viral URTI. He has had three back-to-back salbutamol 12-puff nebulised treatments via spacer, ipratropium 250 mcg nebulised, and IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg in the past 45 minutes. He weighs 22 kg. He remains in marked distress and cannot speak in sentences. There is no allergy history. The team has prepared IV access.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 50, SpO2 91% on 6 L/min facemask, temperature 37.4 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Marked accessory muscle use, widespread wheeze with prolonged expiration, no silent chest
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.30, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 22, lactate 3.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: IV magnesium sulfate is the first-line IV bronchodilator after maximal inhaled therapy plus steroid in paediatric acute severe asthma; bolus is given over 20 minutes with monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute; National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: IV salbutamol 15 mcg/kg loading dose over 10 minutes then infusion
options.rationale: IV salbutamol may be used if IV magnesium fails or is contraindicated; lactataemia from inhaled salbutamol is already evident and starting IV salbutamol first risks toxicity. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: IV aminophylline 5 mg/kg loading dose over 60 minutes
options.rationale: Aminophylline is reserved for failure of magnesium and IV salbutamol in PICU because of its narrow therapeutic index and toxicity; it is not the next step. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Intubate and ventilate using ketamine and rocuronium for rapid sequence
options.rationale: Intubation is a last resort in paediatric asthma because of the risk of dynamic hyperinflation; he has hypercapnia of only 48 mmHg and is not in arrest, so IV therapy is the next step. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute; ANZICS paediatric airway)

options.text: Switch to continuous nebulised salbutamol 10 mg/hour without IV therapy
options.rationale: Continuous nebulised salbutamol may add a marginal amount over intermittent; without escalation to IV magnesium it does not address the trajectory of severe asthma. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

explanation.summary: Acute severe paediatric asthma is treated stepwise: oxygen, inhaled salbutamol back-to-back (with ipratropium added for severe), oral or IV corticosteroid early, then IV magnesium sulfate, then IV salbutamol if failing, then aminophylline in PICU, then intubation as a last resort. Hypercapnia in paediatric asthma is a late sign and a falling pCO2 of less than 35 mmHg becoming normal at 40 mmHg is concerning. Lactataemia is expected from beta-2 agonist use and is not a reason to stop salbutamol.
key_points: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes - first IV agent
IV salbutamol loading dose 15 mcg/kg if magnesium fails
Aminophylline only after both above in PICU
Intubation last resort - high risk of dynamic hyperinflation
Lactataemia is expected from salbutamol and is not a stop signal
explanation.pearls: In paediatric acute severe asthma, a normalising pCO2 (40 mmHg) after maximal therapy is a danger sign - it signals impending respiratory failure, not improvement.

stem: A 22-month-old boy presents to a regional emergency department at 0200 with 6 hours of barking cough, stridor at rest and increased work of breathing. You diagnose moderate to severe croup and give 0.15 mg/kg oral dexamethasone and a single 5 mL nebulised 1:1000 adrenaline dose. Forty-five minutes later he is markedly improved with no stridor at rest, normal effort, and is settled in his mother's arms. The drive to home is 35 minutes and the family is reliable.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118, RR 28, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 37.9 degrees C
data_table.Examination: No stridor at rest, occasional barky cough, normal work of breathing, alert
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Discharge home immediately with safety-netting advice and GP review
options.rationale: Discharge before the post-adrenaline observation window risks rebound stridor at home; adrenaline effect is short and stridor can recur within hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Observe for a further 3 hours after the nebulised adrenaline before discharge
options.rationale: Australian guidelines require observation for 3 to 4 hours after nebulised adrenaline because the effect lasts approximately 2 hours and rebound stridor may occur; if stable at that point, discharge is safe. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup; eTG Paediatrics)

options.text: Admit to the short-stay unit for overnight observation regardless of progress
options.rationale: Mandatory admission overnight is not required if he remains stridor-free at rest 3 to 4 hours after adrenaline; admission decisions are clinical, not routine. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Repeat nebulised adrenaline and observe for 30 minutes before discharge
options.rationale: Repeat adrenaline is given only if stridor recurs; he is currently stridor-free and a routine repeat dose adds no benefit but obscures the observation window. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Administer a second oral dexamethasone dose 0.6 mg/kg and discharge
options.rationale: A second dexamethasone dose is not standard within the same episode; the issue is observation length after adrenaline, not steroid dosing. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for moderate to severe croup and produces transient bronchospasm relief lasting about 2 hours. Australian guidelines require at least 3 hours of observation after the dose, ideally 4 hours, to capture rebound stridor. Dexamethasone is given concurrently and has onset over 2 to 6 hours, so observation also confirms steroid efficacy. Discharge criteria include stridor-free at rest, normal work of breathing, oral intake adequate, and reliable carers within travelling distance of a service.
key_points: Observe for 3 to 4 hours after nebulised adrenaline for rebound stridor
Dexamethasone 0.15 to 0.6 mg/kg PO is the standard steroid dose
Repeat adrenaline only if stridor returns - not prophylactically
Bacterial tracheitis or epiglottitis should be reconsidered if not responsive
explanation.pearls: A child who needs a second dose of nebulised adrenaline within the first 3 hours warrants admission and PICU notification, regardless of how well they look between doses.

stem: A 13-month-old girl presents to a metropolitan ED with 3 days of fever to 39.2 degrees C, cough and tachypnoea. She is fully immunised including 13-valent pneumococcal vaccine and is otherwise well. Vital signs are stable, she has mild hypoxia, and the chest X-ray shows right-sided lobar consolidation. She tolerates oral intake. Mother asks whether she should be on hospital antibiotics or take something home. The closest hospital pharmacy is open. The referring GP queried whether macrolide cover is needed.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 144, RR 48, SpO2 93% room air, temperature 38.9 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Mild subcostal recession, focal crackles right base, no wheeze, alert and feeding
data_table.Investigations: CXR: right lower lobe consolidation, no effusion. CRP 88 mg/L. Nasopharyngeal viral PCR pending.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic plan?

options.text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 5 days as a macrolide-first strategy
options.rationale: Atypical organisms are uncommon causes of CAP in children under 5 years; macrolide-first treatment misses pneumococcal cover that remains the dominant pathogen. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic - paediatric pneumonia)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin + clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Broader gram-negative cover is not indicated for uncomplicated community-acquired pneumonia in an immunised, well-appearing toddler; clavulanate increases diarrhoea risk without benefit. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic - paediatric pneumonia)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days, review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Australian paediatric CAP guidelines recommend amoxicillin as the first-line agent because Streptococcus pneumoniae remains the dominant pathogen; 5 days is sufficient for uncomplicated cases. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic - paediatric pneumonia; AMH Children's Dosing Companion)

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg every 6 hours admitted for 48 hours then oral
options.rationale: IV therapy is reserved for severe CAP, vomiting, or failure of oral therapy; she is feeding and not severely unwell, so oral amoxicillin is appropriate. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic - paediatric pneumonia)

options.text: Oral cefuroxime 15 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days as broader cover
options.rationale: Cefuroxime is not first-line for paediatric CAP in Australia; it has poor pneumococcal penetration at oral doses and broader spectrum is not warranted. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic - paediatric pneumonia)

explanation.summary: Australian paediatric CAP guidance recommends oral amoxicillin as first line for non-severe CAP in fully immunised children, 25 mg/kg three times daily (or twice daily formulation depending on preparation) for 5 days. Pneumococcus remains dominant despite vaccination, and amoxicillin has excellent pneumococcal coverage. Macrolides add atypical cover but atypicals are uncommon under 5 years. IV is reserved for severe disease (SpO2 under 92 percent persistent on oxygen, dehydration, failure of oral therapy). Add macrolide if atypical features (older child, dry cough, headache, gradual onset).
key_points: Amoxicillin 25 mg/kg TDS for 5 days for uncomplicated paediatric CAP
Atypicals are uncommon in under-5s; macrolide-first not indicated
IV penicillin only for severe disease or failure of oral
Add macrolide for atypical features (school age, dry cough, headache)
explanation.pearls: If a child fails 48 hours of oral amoxicillin for CAP, think effusion, empyema or atypical - re-image with ultrasound and add azithromycin only then.

subtopic_detail: Kawasaki disease: incomplete criteria + IVIG timing
stem: A 14-month-old previously well boy of Korean background is brought in on day 6 of fever above 39 degrees C. The fever has not responded to paracetamol. On examination he has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, a strawberry tongue, and an ill-defined truncal rash. He has only one cervical lymph node measuring 1.2 cm and no extremity changes. His hands and feet look normal. Mother has Googled and asks if this could be Kawasaki disease. The locum registrar argues he does not meet 4 of 5 classic criteria, so cannot have Kawasaki disease.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156, RR 30, SpO2 99%, temperature 39.2 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, ill-defined truncal rash, one 1.2 cm cervical node, hands and feet unremarkable, irritable
data_table.Investigations: CRP 95 mg/L, ESR 62 mm/hr, WCC 17.4 x10^9/L (neutrophil predominant), platelets 580 x10^9/L, ALT 52 U/L, albumin 28 g/L, urine WCC 35 x10^6/L sterile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure parents, treat as viral illness, review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Day 6 of fever above 39 degrees C with conjunctivitis, mucocutaneous features and a marked inflammatory profile is high risk; expectant management risks missing the IVIG window for coronary protection. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Arrange echocardiography and give IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg plus aspirin
options.rationale: Incomplete Kawasaki criteria (fever 5 or more days plus 2 to 3 classic features) with elevated CRP and other supportive labs (sterile pyuria, platelet rise, low albumin, raised ALT) meets the AHA/RCH algorithm for treatment; IVIG within 10 days protects coronary arteries. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease; AHA 2017 scientific statement)

options.text: Take blood cultures and start empirical IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily
options.rationale: He has no source of bacterial infection and is well perfused; empirical antibiotics are unlikely to be needed and delay specific therapy for Kawasaki. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Give a single IV methylprednisolone pulse 30 mg/kg as first-line therapy
options.rationale: Steroids may be added for high-risk Kawasaki or IVIG resistance but are not first-line monotherapy in Australian practice; IVIG remains the primary treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Wait until day 10 of fever and re-evaluate the criteria before treating
options.rationale: Delaying treatment until day 10 increases coronary artery aneurysm risk; the algorithm explicitly supports treating incomplete cases earlier when labs and echo support the diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is diagnosed on fever 5 or more days plus 2 or 3 classic features, supported by elevated inflammatory markers and at least 3 of: anaemia, platelets above 450 x10^9/L after day 7, albumin under 30 g/L, ALT elevation, WCC above 15 x10^9/L, sterile pyuria, or coronary changes on echo. IVIG 2 g/kg as a single infusion plus aspirin should be given within 10 days of fever onset to reduce coronary artery aneurysm risk from about 25 percent to under 5 percent. High-risk patients (under 1 year, Asian descent, low albumin, high CRP) may benefit from added corticosteroid (PUFA score 5 or above).
key_points: Treat incomplete Kawasaki on the algorithm - do not wait for 4 of 5 classic features
IVIG 2 g/kg single dose within 10 days of fever onset
Adjunct aspirin (anti-inflammatory then antiplatelet)
Echocardiogram at diagnosis, 2 weeks, 6 to 8 weeks
High-risk groups (under 1 year, Asian, high CRP) may need adjunct steroid
explanation.pearls: Sterile pyuria, ALT above 50 U/L, albumin under 30 g/L, and platelets above 450 x10^9/L after day 7 are the four lab criteria that move an incomplete picture into 'treat'.

stem: A 14-month-old boy has just completed a 7-day course of cephalexin for his first culture-confirmed febrile urinary tract infection caused by Escherichia coli sensitive to amoxicillin and cephalexin. He defervesced within 48 hours of antibiotics. Antenatal scans were normal. He is now well, growing along the 50th centile, voiding normally and feeding well. Mother asks about follow-up imaging because his older brother had a renal scan.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, RR 28, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Well-appearing, abdomen soft and non-tender, no flank tenderness, external genitalia normal
data_table.Investigations: Urine culture pre-treatment: E. coli, sensitive to amoxicillin and cephalexin. Post-treatment urine culture not done.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate follow-up imaging plan?

options.text: Renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks; escalate only if abnormal
options.rationale: Australian guidance recommends a renal tract ultrasound after a first febrile UTI in a child under 24 months; further imaging (DMSA, MCUG) is added only if ultrasound is abnormal, recurrence occurs, or atypical features arise. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI; KHA-CARI)

options.text: Micturating cystourethrogram now and DMSA scan at 6 months
options.rationale: MCUG and DMSA are not routine after a single typical UTI that responded to treatment in a well child; both involve radiation and invasive instrumentation. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months to assess for renal scarring
options.rationale: DMSA is reserved for atypical or recurrent infections, abnormal ultrasound or high suspicion of reflux nephropathy; a single typical UTI does not require it. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: Daily trimethoprim prophylaxis with no further imaging arranged
options.rationale: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis after a single uncomplicated febrile UTI is not recommended and imaging is still indicated under 24 months. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: No imaging required; review only if recurrence occurs
options.rationale: A renal ultrasound is recommended even after a single febrile UTI in a child under 24 months to screen for structural anomalies. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

explanation.summary: After a first febrile UTI in a child under 24 months, the Australian approach is renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks. If ultrasound is normal and the UTI was typical (good response to first-line antibiotics, no septicaemia, no non-E coli organism), no further imaging is required. DMSA is added for recurrent UTI, atypical features (non-E coli, severe illness, slow response), or abnormal ultrasound. MCUG is reserved for marked hydronephrosis on ultrasound, suspected posterior urethral valves, or recurrent UTI under 6 months.
key_points: First febrile UTI under 24 months - ultrasound within 6 weeks
DMSA only for recurrence or atypical features
MCUG only for marked hydronephrosis or PUV suspicion
Routine antibiotic prophylaxis not recommended after single uncomplicated UTI
Counsel parents on prompt presentation for future fevers
explanation.pearls: A first UTI in an infant boy is the indication that triggers the strongest imaging cascade - if his ultrasound shows hydronephrosis above 10 mm, escalate to MCUG to exclude posterior urethral valves.

stem: A 4-year-old girl is admitted with viral gastroenteritis and has been on 0.45 percent saline plus 5 percent dextrose maintenance for the past 18 hours. On the morning ward round she has a generalised tonic-clonic seizure that does not stop after 90 seconds. She has had 4 episodes of vomiting overnight. Her admission sodium was 137 mmol/L. The bedside finger-prick glucose is 5.2 mmol/L. IV access is patent. She weighs 16 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158, RR 36, SpO2 95% on 4 L/min Hudson mask, temperature 36.9 degrees C, BP 92/56
data_table.Examination: Ongoing tonic-clonic seizure, pupils 3 mm equal, no focal features, mottled extremities
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.32, Na 122 mmol/L, K 3.8, glucose 5.2 mmol/L, lactate 2.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg bolus over 20 minutes
options.rationale: Isotonic saline raises sodium too slowly in symptomatic hyponatraemia and large volumes risk further dilution if SIADH is contributing. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyponatraemia)

options.text: IV midazolam 0.15 mg/kg and switch maintenance to 0.9% saline
options.rationale: Benzodiazepine alone treats the seizure symptom but does not address the underlying acute hyponatraemia driving it; ongoing low sodium will re-seize. (Source: RCH CPG: Seizure)

options.text: IV 3% sodium chloride 3 mL/kg bolus over 10 minutes
options.rationale: Acute symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizure) requires immediate sodium correction with hypertonic 3 percent saline 2 to 5 mL/kg bolus; this raises serum sodium acutely to abort the seizure and reduce cerebral oedema. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyponatraemia; ANZICS)

options.text: IV mannitol 0.5 g/kg over 20 minutes for cerebral oedema
options.rationale: Mannitol is not first line in hyponatraemic cerebral oedema; the driver is low effective osmolality and the answer is to raise sodium with hypertonic saline. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyponatraemia)

options.text: IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg load and continue current maintenance fluid
options.rationale: Phenytoin is a second-line anticonvulsant; in hyponatraemic seizure the priority is correcting sodium with hypertonic saline. (Source: RCH CPG: Seizure)

explanation.summary: Hospital-acquired hyponatraemia in children remains a recognised hazard of hypotonic maintenance fluids in the setting of stress-induced ADH release. Australian and international paediatric guidance now recommends isotonic maintenance (0.9 percent saline plus 5 percent dextrose) for most acutely unwell children. Symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizure, depressed conscious state) requires immediate 3 percent sodium chloride 2 to 5 mL/kg bolus. The aim is to raise serum sodium by 4 to 6 mmol/L acutely to resolve symptoms; thereafter correct slowly over 24 hours.
key_points: Use isotonic maintenance (0.9% NaCl + 5% glucose) in acutely unwell children
Symptomatic hyponatraemia: 3% NaCl 2 to 5 mL/kg bolus
Aim to raise Na by 4 to 6 mmol/L acutely to abort symptoms
Avoid raising Na by more than 8 to 10 mmol/L in 24 hours (osmotic demyelination)
Investigate cause (SIADH, gastro losses, water intoxication)
explanation.pearls: In an unwell child with hyponatraemic seizure, give 3% sodium chloride 2 mL/kg bolus and recheck the sodium in 15 minutes - the seizure stops almost immediately as Na rises 3 to 5 mmol/L.

stem: A 7-month-old girl is brought to a tertiary emergency department after a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes that occurred 90 minutes ago at home during a febrile illness. She is fully immunised including pneumococcal and Hib vaccines. She is currently alert and feeding. Her admission temperature is 39.0 degrees C. There is no rash, neck stiffness or bulging fontanelle. She has not been on antibiotics. The intern proposes a lumbar puncture; the registrar disagrees.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156, RR 36, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 39.0 degrees C, GCS 15
data_table.Examination: Alert, feeding, anterior fontanelle soft and flat, no neck stiffness, no rash, no focal neurology, no petechiae
data_table.Investigations: Capillary glucose 5.4 mmol/L, urine dipstick pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture immediately before further treatment is started
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture is no longer mandatory in a fully immunised infant over 6 months after a simple febrile seizure who is well-appearing; selective LP is the current approach. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure; AAP)

options.text: Observe for 4 hours and perform LP if signs of meningitis emerge
options.rationale: Selective LP is recommended for first simple febrile seizure in a fully immunised infant over 6 months without signs of CNS infection; observe for clinical change and do LP only if features develop. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Start empirical IV ceftriaxone and discharge after 1 hour
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics for a presumed simple febrile seizure without features of bacterial infection are not indicated and may obscure later diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Arrange urgent CT head to exclude raised intracranial pressure
options.rationale: CT head is not indicated in a simple febrile seizure with normal neurology and conscious state; the seizure was brief, generalised and non-focal. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

options.text: Discharge home with safety-net advice and antipyretic plan immediately
options.rationale: Immediate discharge after a first febrile seizure without an observation period and parental education risks early re-presentation and misses a window to identify atypical features. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizure)

explanation.summary: A simple febrile seizure is generalised, lasts under 15 minutes, has no recurrence within 24 hours and occurs between 6 months and 5 years. In a fully immunised, alert child without signs of meningitis, lumbar puncture is not routine. LP is indicated for signs of meningitis (bulging fontanelle, neck stiffness, persistent altered mental state), complex febrile seizure (focal, prolonged, recurrent within 24 hours), pretreatment with antibiotics that could mask meningitis, or age under 6 months. Observation of at least 1 to 4 hours allows clinical reassessment and parental education before discharge.
key_points: Simple febrile seizure: 6 months to 5 years, less than 15 min, generalised, single in 24 hours
LP not routine if fully immunised and well-appearing
LP for complex features, signs of meningitis, age under 6 months, or pretreatment with antibiotics
Antipyretics do not prevent febrile seizures
Recurrence risk ~30% lifetime
explanation.pearls: For first simple febrile seizure in a fully immunised infant over 6 months, the LP decision is driven by examination at hour 1, not by reflex on arrival - re-examine before deciding.

stem: A 4-year-old boy with a known structural focal epilepsy is brought to a metropolitan ED in a generalised convulsive seizure that has lasted 12 minutes. He received 0.5 mg/kg buccal midazolam from his mother at home 9 minutes ago and a further 0.15 mg/kg IV midazolam 5 minutes ago without effect. He weighs 16 kg. IV access is patent. He is hypoxic on facemask oxygen. There is no history of cardiac disease or pre-existing QT prolongation. The team are preparing the next IV agent.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 162, RR 28 irregular, SpO2 91% on 8 L/min facemask, temperature 37.4 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Ongoing tonic-clonic activity, eyes deviated right, no focal lateralisation, pupils 4 mm equal sluggish
data_table.Investigations: Capillary glucose 5.6 mmol/L, ABG drawn

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next agent?

options.text: IV levetiracetam 40 mg/kg infusion over 5 minutes
options.rationale: After two doses of benzodiazepine fail, IV levetiracetam 40 to 60 mg/kg is now first-line second-line therapy in Australian paediatric status epilepticus, with non-inferior efficacy to phenytoin and a better safety profile. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus; ConSEPT/EcLiPSE trials)

options.text: IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg loading dose over 20 minutes
options.rationale: Phenytoin remains an acceptable second-line but levetiracetam is now preferred because of equivalent efficacy and fewer cardiac side effects; phenytoin is reserved when levetiracetam fails or is contraindicated. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Repeat IV midazolam 0.15 mg/kg as a third dose
options.rationale: More than two benzodiazepine doses increase the risk of respiratory depression without improving seizure termination; move to a non-benzodiazepine second-line agent. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

options.text: IV thiopentone 4 mg/kg with rapid sequence intubation
options.rationale: Anaesthetic induction with intubation is reserved for refractory status (greater than 30 minutes despite two second-line agents) and requires PICU; it is not the immediate next step. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

options.text: IV sodium valproate 40 mg/kg over 5 minutes
options.rationale: Valproate is an alternative second-line but is avoided in children under 2 years and contraindicated in suspected metabolic disease; levetiracetam is the preferred first second-line in Australia. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

explanation.summary: Paediatric status epilepticus algorithms in Australia use stepwise treatment: benzodiazepine (midazolam or lorazepam) at 5 minutes and 10 minutes, then non-benzodiazepine second-line agent at 15 to 20 minutes, then induction with intubation if refractory beyond 30 minutes. ConSEPT (Australia, NZ) and EcLiPSE (UK) trials showed levetiracetam and phenytoin have equivalent efficacy, with levetiracetam preferred for ease of administration, fewer cardiac side effects, and absence of QT prolongation risk. Loading dose 40 to 60 mg/kg over 5 minutes.
key_points: Two benzodiazepine doses maximum before second-line agent
Levetiracetam 40 to 60 mg/kg over 5 minutes - new first second-line in Australia
Phenytoin 20 mg/kg over 20 minutes alternative if levetiracetam fails
Refractory status (more than 30 min) needs anaesthetic induction and PICU
Avoid valproate in under-2s and in metabolic disease
explanation.pearls: Levetiracetam 40 to 60 mg/kg IV over 5 minutes is now the first second-line agent for paediatric status epilepticus - phenytoin only if levetiracetam is unavailable or has failed.

stem: A breastfed term male newborn (40 weeks, birthweight 3.4 kg) is reviewed at 60 hours of life on the postnatal ward. He has visible jaundice to the upper chest. Antenatal blood group was O Rhesus positive; baby's group has not been checked. There is no family history of haemolytic disease. He is feeding 3 hourly and has had 4 wet nappies in the past 24 hours. Total serum bilirubin (TSB) returns at 312 micromol/L. The local phototherapy threshold chart shows the 60-hour line for a low-risk term infant at 320 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 142, RR 44, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.9 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Visible jaundice to upper chest, alert, good tone, suck strong, no hepatosplenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: TSB 312 micromol/L (conjugated 8 micromol/L), Hb 178 g/L, blood group not yet determined, DAT pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence phototherapy immediately and recheck TSB in 6 hours
options.rationale: TSB is just below the phototherapy threshold; starting phototherapy without rechecking risks unnecessary mother-infant separation and feeding disruption. The trend matters more than the single value. (Source: RWH/RCH Neonatal jaundice guideline)

options.text: Discharge home with community midwife review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Discharge without rechecking TSB when the level is only 8 micromol/L below threshold risks crossing the threshold unobserved at home. (Source: RWH/RCH Neonatal jaundice guideline)

options.text: Arrange immediate exchange transfusion in the next 12 hours
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion thresholds are typically 80 to 100 micromol/L above phototherapy and reserved for severe hyperbilirubinaemia; this TSB is well below. (Source: RWH/RCH Neonatal jaundice guideline)

options.text: Switch from breast milk to formula and recheck TSB in 24 hours
options.rationale: Routine formula substitution is not recommended; breastfeeding support and re-evaluation is preferred even when phototherapy is indicated. (Source: RWH/RCH Neonatal jaundice guideline)

options.text: Recheck TSB in 6 to 12 hours and reassess against threshold
options.rationale: TSB is 8 micromol/L below the threshold for a low-risk term infant at 60 hours; the standard practice is to recheck in 6 to 12 hours and treat if the trajectory crosses the line. Investigate aetiology if rising. (Source: RWH/RCH Neonatal jaundice guideline)

explanation.summary: Australian neonatal jaundice guidance uses age-in-hours bilirubin thresholds plotted on phototherapy and exchange transfusion nomograms, accounting for risk factors (prematurity, haemolysis, sepsis, low albumin). For a low-risk term infant the 60-hour phototherapy line sits around 320 micromol/L. Values close to but below the line trigger a 6 to 12 hour recheck. Always check for aetiology when bilirubin rises faster than 5 micromol/L per hour, total exceeds the 95th centile for age, or conjugated fraction is above 20 micromol/L (which always needs work-up).
key_points: Use age-in-hours nomogram - not absolute thresholds
Rising TSB by more than 5 micromol/L per hour warrants investigation
Conjugated bilirubin above 20 micromol/L always warrants work-up
Maintain breastfeeding alongside phototherapy
Exchange transfusion thresholds typically 80 to 100 micromol/L above phototherapy
explanation.pearls: Conjugated (direct) bilirubin above 20 micromol/L is never physiological and demands biliary atresia work-up; check stool colour and refer urgently if pale.

stem: A 3-year-old boy weighing 16 kg is brought to a metropolitan ED 4 hours after his mother saw him chewing ferrous sulfate 325 mg tablets from her bedside table. She estimates he ate between 10 and 15 tablets. He has vomited twice and had 1 episode of bloody diarrhoea. A 4-hour serum iron level returns at 92 micromol/L. Anion gap is 22. The poisons information centre has been notified. IV access is patent.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 162, RR 32, BP 84/52, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 37.1 degrees C, CRT 4 seconds
data_table.Examination: Pale, peripheries cool, lethargic but rousable, abdomen tender, no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: Serum iron 92 micromol/L at 4 hours, VBG pH 7.27 lactate 5.4 mmol/L, anion gap 22, glucose 4.8 mmol/L, abdominal X-ray: scattered radio-opaque tablets in stomach

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: IV desferrioxamine 15 mg/kg/hour titrated for 24 hours
options.rationale: Serum iron above 90 micromol/L with metabolic acidosis and systemic toxicity is an indication for IV desferrioxamine chelation; start at 15 mg/kg/hour and titrate. (Source: Poisons Information Centre - Iron; eTG Toxicology)

options.text: Activated charcoal 1 g/kg orally and observe for 24 hours
options.rationale: Activated charcoal does not bind iron and is not indicated in iron overdose; relying on it would miss the window for chelation. (Source: Poisons Information Centre - Iron; eTG Toxicology)

options.text: Gastric lavage and oral N-acetylcysteine for 21 hours
options.rationale: Lavage 4 hours post ingestion has poor yield and NAC is for paracetamol, not iron; this misses the chelation indication. (Source: Poisons Information Centre - Iron)

options.text: Whole-bowel irrigation only and recheck iron at 8 hours
options.rationale: Whole-bowel irrigation may be an adjunct for radio-opaque tablets but does not replace chelation when systemic toxicity and high iron levels are present. (Source: Poisons Information Centre - Iron)

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate infusion to correct acidaemia
options.rationale: Bicarbonate does not address the iron toxin itself; acidosis is a marker of cellular injury and will improve with chelation, not isolated alkalinisation. (Source: Poisons Information Centre - Iron)

explanation.summary: Iron overdose in children proceeds through five clinical stages: GI (first 6 hours), latent (6 to 24 hours), shock and metabolic acidosis (24 to 48 hours), hepatotoxicity (2 to 4 days), and late GI scarring (4 to 6 weeks). A 4-hour serum iron level above 90 micromol/L predicts systemic toxicity and is the threshold for IV desferrioxamine. Other indications include severe symptoms (haematemesis, altered mental status, persistent vomiting), metabolic acidosis, or unknown level with substantial ingestion. Treat for at least 24 hours; urine turns red-orange (vin rosé) as iron is excreted.
key_points: 4-hour serum iron above 90 micromol/L = chelation
Activated charcoal does NOT bind iron
Whole-bowel irrigation as adjunct for visible tablets on AXR
Desferrioxamine 15 mg/kg/hour IV, max 6 g/24 h
Stop chelation when urine clears and patient improves
explanation.pearls: A 4-hour serum iron above 90 micromol/L mandates IV desferrioxamine regardless of how well the child looks - the latent phase will end in shock if untreated.

stem: A 5-year-old boy weighing 20 kg presents to a regional ED 35 minutes after eating cashew nuts at a birthday party. He developed urticaria, lip swelling and wheeze within minutes. He received IM adrenaline 0.15 mg from a school EpiPen Junior 15 minutes ago. On arrival you give a second IM adrenaline 0.3 mg into the lateral thigh, supine positioning, oxygen, and IV fluid 20 mL/kg. He now has resolved wheeze, no stridor, and normal effort. His parents are asking when they can go home; the trip is 90 minutes by car.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118, BP 102/64, RR 26, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.8 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Residual mild lip swelling, no wheeze, no stridor, no rash now, alert and engaging
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated acutely

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition plan?

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days and discharge
options.rationale: Oral steroids are not recommended routinely after anaphylaxis and do not justify shortening observation. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis guidelines)

options.text: Discharge home now with a new EpiPen prescription and GP follow-up
options.rationale: Discharge soon after the second adrenaline dose does not allow biphasic reaction observation; ASCIA recommends a minimum 4-hour monitored period in this higher-risk group. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis guidelines)

options.text: Admit overnight for 24-hour observation regardless of clinical course
options.rationale: Routine overnight admission is not required if the child remains well at 4 hours; clinical reassessment guides discharge. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis guidelines)

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg and discharge in 1 hour
options.rationale: Steroids do not reduce biphasic reactions in the evidence base and are not a basis for early discharge. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis guidelines)

options.text: Observe in monitored bed for at least 4 hours from second adrenaline dose
options.rationale: Patients who require more than one adrenaline dose are at higher risk of biphasic reactions and require a minimum 4-hour observation from the last adrenaline; ASCIA recommends this group are managed as higher risk. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis guidelines)

explanation.summary: ASCIA recommends a minimum 4-hour observation after IM adrenaline for anaphylaxis, extended for higher-risk patients including those requiring more than one adrenaline dose, severe presentations, asthmatics, history of biphasic reactions, ongoing allergen exposure, or limited access to emergency care. Biphasic reactions occur in approximately 5 percent of paediatric anaphylaxis cases, typically within 1 to 4 hours but up to 24 hours after initial resolution. Discharge planning includes a personalised ASCIA action plan, two adrenaline auto-injectors, allergy clinic referral, and clear safety-netting.
key_points: Standard observation 4 hours from last adrenaline dose
Higher-risk patients: more than one adrenaline dose, severe, asthma, biphasic history
Two EpiPens on discharge with ASCIA action plan
Allergy clinic referral for definitive testing
Steroids and antihistamines are adjunctive only
explanation.pearls: Any child who needed two IM adrenaline doses is in the higher-risk group - keep monitored for at least 4 hours from the second dose before discharge.

subtopic_detail: Otitis media: antibiotic threshold in Aboriginal child
stem: A 2-year-old Aboriginal boy living in a remote Northern Territory community is brought to the community clinic with 36 hours of fever, ear pulling, and irritability. He has had two previous episodes of acute otitis media this year. Both tympanic membranes are red and bulging, and the left is now perforated with mucopurulent discharge. He is otherwise well-perfused. His weight is 12 kg. The community-controlled health organisation has telephone access to a remote paediatrician.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130, RR 28, SpO2 98%, temperature 38.6 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Bilateral red bulging TMs, left perforation with discharge, no mastoid tenderness, no neck stiffness, mild rhinorrhoea
data_table.Investigations: Tympanocentesis swab obtained from discharging ear

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Watchful waiting with paracetamol; review at 48 hours
options.rationale: Watchful waiting is not appropriate for Aboriginal children, in whom otitis media has a higher risk of progression to chronic suppurative otitis media and hearing loss; immediate antibiotics are recommended. (Source: NACCHO/CARPA Standard Treatment Manual)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days, then review
options.rationale: Aboriginal children with acute otitis media should receive immediate high-dose amoxicillin for 7 days because of the higher risk of perforation, persistent suppuration and hearing loss compared to non-Aboriginal children. (Source: NACCHO/CARPA Standard Treatment Manual; eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Topical ciprofloxacin ear drops alone for the perforated ear
options.rationale: Topical ciprofloxacin is an adjunct in chronic suppurative otitis media but does not replace systemic antibiotics in acute otitis media with perforation in a high-risk child. (Source: NACCHO/CARPA Standard Treatment Manual)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Augmentin is reserved for treatment failure or recurrence within 4 weeks; high-dose amoxicillin remains the first-line agent in remote Aboriginal communities. (Source: NACCHO/CARPA Standard Treatment Manual)

options.text: Single IM ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg with no oral follow-on course
options.rationale: Single-dose IM ceftriaxone is used when oral therapy is not feasible; here oral amoxicillin is appropriate and 7 days is needed for full course. (Source: NACCHO/CARPA Standard Treatment Manual)

explanation.summary: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children, particularly in remote communities, have markedly higher rates of acute otitis media with perforation and chronic suppurative otitis media, with consequent hearing loss, language delay and learning impact. Australian remote-community guidelines (CARPA Standard Treatment Manual) recommend immediate high-dose amoxicillin (30 mg/kg BD) for 7 days for any acute otitis media episode in this population. Watchful waiting is reserved for non-Aboriginal children over 2 years with unilateral non-severe disease. Follow-up at 7 days is essential to assess resolution.
key_points: Aboriginal children with AOM: immediate amoxicillin, no watchful waiting
High-dose amoxicillin 30 mg/kg BD for 7 days
Follow-up at day 7 - if persistent perforation, treat as chronic suppurative otitis media
Topical ciprofloxacin drops added if discharge persists at 6 weeks (CSOM)
Screen siblings; coordinate ENT and audiology referral
explanation.pearls: In remote Aboriginal communities, an AOM perforation that persists past 6 weeks is chronic suppurative otitis media - escalate to topical ciprofloxacin ear toilet and ENT referral.

stem: A 12-year-old boy with a BMI on the 97th centile is brought to a metropolitan ED with 6 weeks of intermittent left thigh and knee pain, worse in the past 4 days. He has not had a fever. He can weight-bear with a limp. On examination he holds the left leg in external rotation; passive internal rotation of the left hip is restricted. Plain radiographs (AP and frog-leg lateral) of both hips show widening of the left physis and a Klein line that does not intersect the femoral head epiphysis. He has no headaches and his blood pressure is normal for age.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 86, BP 124/78, RR 18, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Externally rotated left lower limb, restricted internal rotation, no warmth or erythema, no effusion clinically, intact distal neurovascular status
data_table.Investigations: AP and frog-leg lateral pelvis - widened left physis with Klein line not intersecting epiphysis, no fracture

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with crutches and orthopaedic clinic review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Outpatient management risks slip progression and femoral head avascular necrosis; SUFE is an urgent orthopaedic admission requiring strict non-weight-bearing and in situ fixation. (Source: RCH CPG: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis)

options.text: Admit, strict non-weight-bearing, urgent orthopaedic surgery review
options.rationale: SUFE is a surgical emergency once diagnosed; the patient must be made strict non-weight-bearing and referred urgently for in situ pinning to prevent progression and avascular necrosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis)

options.text: MRI hip electively to characterise the slip before referral
options.rationale: Plain radiographs are diagnostic; MRI delays definitive surgical management without changing immediate treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: SUFE)

options.text: Trial of weight-bearing rest, analgesia and review in 1 week
options.rationale: Continued weight-bearing risks slip progression and AVN; SUFE patients must be non-weight-bearing from diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: SUFE)

options.text: Aspirate the hip joint to exclude underlying septic arthritis
options.rationale: He has no fever, no warmth, no inflammatory markers documented, and a Klein line consistent with SUFE; aspiration is not the next step. (Source: RCH CPG: SUFE)

explanation.summary: SUFE typically occurs in adolescents aged 10 to 16 years, more commonly male, obese or with endocrinopathy (hypothyroidism, growth hormone deficiency, hypogonadism). Knee or thigh pain is a classic referred pattern that delays diagnosis. The Klein line drawn along the superior femoral neck on AP view should intersect the femoral head epiphysis; failure to do so signals SUFE. Once diagnosed, the patient must not weight-bear, and urgent orthopaedic referral for in situ pinning is essential to prevent progression to chronic SUFE or avascular necrosis. The contralateral hip is at risk of subsequent SUFE in about 20 to 25 percent of cases.
key_points: Adolescent knee/thigh pain - examine the hip
Klein line failing to intersect epiphysis = SUFE
Strict non-weight-bearing from diagnosis
Urgent orthopaedic in situ pinning
Screen for endocrinopathy if atypical (under 10 yrs, very thin)
explanation.pearls: Any adolescent with knee or thigh pain and externally rotated leg gets an AP plus frog-leg lateral pelvis - and stays on the bed until orthopaedics reviews.

stem: A 5-year-old previously well boy is brought to a metropolitan ED with 36 hours of right hip pain and refusal to weight-bear. He had a viral URTI 1 week ago that has now resolved. His mother gave him paracetamol overnight; the fever has reduced. On examination he holds the hip in mild flexion and external rotation, and resists log-roll. The orthopaedic registrar asks for blood tests before any imaging.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124, RR 24, SpO2 99%, temperature 38.4 degrees C now (was 38.8 overnight)
data_table.Examination: Right hip held flexed and externally rotated, log-roll painful, no overlying erythema, no other joints involved
data_table.Investigations: WCC 13.5 x10^9/L, CRP 38 mg/L, ESR 48 mm/hr

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with NSAIDs and orthopaedic review at 48 hours
options.rationale: He meets 4 of 4 Kocher criteria (fever above 38.5, non-weight-bearing, ESR above 40, WCC above 12) which gives over 90 percent probability of septic arthritis; discharge is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic arthritis)

options.text: Start empirical IV flucloxacillin and discharge in 24 hours
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics without aspiration both impedes culture yield and risks discharge of an unrecognised septic joint that needs washout. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic arthritis)

options.text: Hip ultrasound and urgent orthopaedic review for joint aspiration
options.rationale: With all 4 Kocher criteria positive, the post-test probability of septic arthritis is over 90 percent and definitive diagnosis requires ultrasound-guided aspiration; orthopaedic team must be involved urgently. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic arthritis; Kocher 1999)

options.text: MRI pelvis as the first imaging investigation undertaken
options.rationale: MRI is useful when ultrasound is equivocal or osteomyelitis is suspected, but ultrasound is faster, cheaper and the first-line investigation here. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic arthritis)

options.text: Reassure as transient synovitis and arrange home physiotherapy
options.rationale: Kocher criteria of 4/4 makes transient synovitis very unlikely; missing a septic joint risks joint destruction and sepsis. (Source: RCH CPG: Septic arthritis)

explanation.summary: The Kocher criteria for differentiating septic arthritis from transient synovitis in a child with hip pain are: fever above 38.5 degrees C, non-weight-bearing on the affected side, ESR above 40 mm/hr, and WCC above 12 x10^9/L. Probability of septic arthritis rises from 3 percent at 0 criteria to over 93 percent at 4. CRP above 20 mg/L is a useful supplementary marker. Ultrasound demonstrates effusion; aspiration confirms diagnosis with cell count, Gram stain and culture. Treatment is urgent surgical washout plus IV antibiotics (flucloxacillin or cefazolin) targeting Staphylococcus aureus and Kingella kingae in younger children.
key_points: Kocher 4/4 = over 90% probability of septic arthritis
Ultrasound first imaging, aspiration confirms
Empirical IV flucloxacillin (or vancomycin if MRSA risk)
Surgical washout for confirmed septic arthritis
Differentiate from osteomyelitis with MRI if no effusion on US
explanation.pearls: Kingella kingae causes culture-negative septic arthritis in toddlers - ask the lab to inoculate joint fluid into a paediatric blood culture bottle for highest yield.

stem: A 5-week-old male first-born infant is admitted with 10 days of progressive non-bilious vomiting after feeds. He weighs 3.8 kg (birthweight 3.5 kg). His mother reports a palpable 'olive' was felt during the abdominal examination. Ultrasound confirms pyloric channel length 18 mm and muscle thickness 5 mm. The paediatric surgical registrar plans pyloromyotomy. The anaesthetic team has asked you to optimise him pre-operatively. He has had no urine output in the past 4 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 36, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.6 degrees C, BP 78/48
data_table.Examination: Mildly sunken fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, CRT 3 sec, abdomen soft, no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: Na 132 mmol/L, K 2.8, Cl 88, HCO3 36, urea 9.4, creatinine 42, glucose 4.8, VBG pH 7.52 pCO2 50

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Proceed directly to pyloromyotomy within the next two hours
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis is a medical emergency until electrolytes are corrected, not a surgical emergency; anaesthesia in metabolic alkalosis risks post-operative apnoea and arrhythmia. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: IV 0.9% saline bolus then K-containing maintenance; surgery once Cl above 100
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis is a medical-then-surgical problem; rehydration with 0.9 percent saline, then maintenance with potassium-containing fluid, correcting hypochloraemic hypokalaemic alkalosis until Cl over 100 and HCO3 under 30 before anaesthesia, is standard Australian practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: IV Hartmann's solution at maintenance with theatre scheduled in 12 hours
options.rationale: Hartmann's contains lactate that is metabolised to bicarbonate and will worsen the alkalosis; 0.9 percent saline is preferred for resuscitation. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Acetazolamide 5 mg/kg to pharmacologically correct the metabolic alkalosis
options.rationale: Pharmacological alkalosis correction is unnecessary and risks further electrolyte disturbance; volume and chloride replacement is the standard approach. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Nasogastric thickened feeding with review at 48 hours and home discharge
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis is a mechanical obstruction; feeding via NG does not bypass the pyloric channel and delays definitive treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

explanation.summary: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis presents at 3 to 8 weeks with progressive non-bilious projectile vomiting after feeds. Hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis arises from loss of HCl in vomit, aldosterone-driven potassium loss, and chloride-responsive renal bicarbonate retention. Pre-operative fluid correction is essential to reduce anaesthetic risk of apnoea and arrhythmia. Standard targets are chloride above 100 mmol/L, bicarbonate under 30 mmol/L, potassium above 3.5 mmol/L, and adequate urine output. Pyloromyotomy is then performed laparoscopically.
key_points: Pyloric stenosis: 3 to 8 weeks, non-bilious projectile vomiting
Hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis
Resuscitate with 0.9% saline; maintenance with K-containing fluid
Surgery only when Cl above 100, HCO3 under 30, K above 3.5
Pyloromyotomy is curative, feeding restored within 24 hours
explanation.pearls: In pyloric stenosis the operation can wait - the electrolytes cannot; aim chloride above 100 mmol/L and bicarbonate under 30 mmol/L before calling theatre.

stem: A 10-month-old boy presents to a tertiary paediatric ED with 14 hours of intermittent inconsolable crying, drawing his legs up, and 2 episodes of redcurrant jelly stool. He is currently well-perfused, has had 4 wet nappies overnight, but is pale and drawn. Abdominal examination shows a tender right upper quadrant sausage-shaped mass; the right iliac fossa feels empty. Ultrasound shows a target sign at the hepatic flexure with no free fluid and no signs of perforation. He has not had a fever or vomiting. Surgical and radiology teams are available on-site.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 32, BP 92/58, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.9 degrees C, CRT 2 seconds
data_table.Examination: RUQ sausage-shaped mass, no peritonism, no bruises, no abdominal distension, alert when not in pain
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, lactate 1.2 mmol/L, abdominal US: target sign at hepatic flexure, no free fluid, no perforation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Conservative management with NG decompression and IV fluids only
options.rationale: Spontaneous reduction is rare and conservative management risks ischaemic complications; intussusception requires active reduction. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Laparotomy and manual reduction without attempted air enema
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for failed enema, peritonism, perforation, or pathological lead point; air enema should be attempted first in stable children. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Barium enema reduction without surgical team on standby
options.rationale: Air enema is preferred to barium because of lower perforation risk and avoidance of barium peritonitis; surgical standby is also mandatory. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Air enema reduction under fluoroscopy with surgical standby
options.rationale: Air enema is the first-line management of ileocolic intussusception in a haemodynamically stable child without peritonism or perforation; it is successful in 80 to 90 percent and lower-radiation than barium. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: CT abdomen with IV contrast to confirm before any intervention
options.rationale: Ultrasound is already diagnostic with the target sign; CT adds radiation without changing management. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

explanation.summary: Intussusception in children aged 3 months to 3 years is most commonly ileocolic and idiopathic, often after viral infection (lymphoid hyperplasia of Peyer's patches). Classic triad of intermittent abdominal pain, vomiting, and redcurrant jelly stool is present in fewer than 25 percent; ultrasound target sign is the diagnostic test of choice. Air enema reduction under fluoroscopy with surgical standby is the first-line treatment in a stable child without peritonism or perforation. Recurrence rate is 10 percent, mostly within 24 hours. Lead points (Meckel's, polyp, lymphoma) should be considered in children under 3 months or over 5 years, or after recurrence.
key_points: US target sign is diagnostic
Air enema first line - 80 to 90% success
Contraindications to enema: peritonism, perforation, shock
Recurrence 10%, most in 24 hours - keep monitored
Pathological lead point in atypical age or recurrence
explanation.pearls: Hartmann's, NG decompression and a surgical team standing by means safe air enema - the actual definitive reduction is by paediatric radiology.

stem: A 5-month-old boy is brought to a metropolitan ED by his mother with 12 hours of irritability and one episode of vomiting. There is no history of trauma. On examination he has a posterior left torus skull fracture identified on CT brain after the registrar noticed a small bruise on the scalp. He has a healing right tibial fracture identified incidentally. His parents have provided inconsistent accounts of how the bruise occurred. He is otherwise well-perfused and feeding. There are no overlying skin marks suggestive of birthmarks, and Mongolian spots are absent.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 32, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.8 degrees C, alert
data_table.Examination: Healing right tibial fracture identified incidentally, small left scalp bruise, posterior left skull fracture on CT, no retinal haemorrhages yet examined
data_table.Investigations: FBC and coagulation normal, CT brain: torus skull fracture, no intracranial bleed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge home with paediatric outpatient follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Multiple unexplained injuries in an infant warrant mandatory child protection notification and full work-up; discharge would breach mandatory reporting obligations. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse; Australian state mandatory reporting)

options.text: Order coagulation studies only and re-review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Coagulation studies alone do not exclude non-accidental injury; multiple injuries with inconsistent history require full work-up and notification. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse)

options.text: Discuss with the family alone before notifying child protection
options.rationale: Notification is mandatory and is not contingent on parental agreement; delaying to negotiate risks the child's safety. (Source: Australian Mandatory Reporting)

options.text: Admit, notify child protection, skeletal survey and ophthalmology
options.rationale: Multiple unexplained injuries in a non-mobile infant trigger mandatory reporting; full work-up includes skeletal survey, ophthalmology for retinal haemorrhages, repeat coagulation studies, and admission for safe assessment. (Source: RCH CPG: Child abuse; Australian state mandatory reporting)

options.text: Refer to outpatient social work without admission or notification
options.rationale: Outpatient referral does not meet the statutory threshold for mandatory reporting and risks ongoing harm; admission and notification are required now. (Source: Australian Mandatory Reporting)

explanation.summary: Australian doctors are mandatory reporters of suspected child abuse in all states and territories. Concerning features include injuries inconsistent with history, multiple injuries at different stages, injuries in non-mobile infants (rolling at 4 to 6 months, sitting at 6 months), and delayed presentation. Workup includes skeletal survey (with repeat at 10 to 14 days), ophthalmology examination for retinal haemorrhages, abdominal imaging if bruising or markers, coagulation profile, neuroimaging if any signs, and full social work assessment. Admission ensures safety while child protection investigation occurs. Document objectively; do not confront parents about suspicion.
key_points: Injury in a non-mobile infant - presume NAI until excluded
Mandatory reporting to state child protection
Workup: skeletal survey, ophthalmology, coagulation, neuroimaging
Admission for safe assessment - do not discharge
Document factually; avoid confronting parents
explanation.pearls: Any injury in an infant under 6 months who is non-mobile (not rolling) demands a full NAI workup and admission - the differential of accidental trauma is vanishingly small.

stem: A 9-year-old boy with congenital chronic kidney disease (eGFR 28) presents to a metropolitan ED with weakness and palpitations. His ECG shows peaked T waves and widening QRS at 130 ms with no P waves visible. His last potassium 3 weeks ago was 5.4 mmol/L. He weighs 28 kg and is on captopril and a low-potassium diet. Mother reports he ate a banana and orange juice for breakfast. IV access is patent.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92 (irregular), RR 22, BP 118/72, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Reduced peripheral tone, alert, no focal neurology, no oedema
data_table.Investigations: Urgent VBG K 8.2 mmol/L, Na 134, ionised calcium 1.10, glucose 5.6 mmol/L, lactate 1.4, ECG: peaked T waves, QRS 130 ms, no P waves

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first intervention?

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg without IV intervention
options.rationale: Salbutamol lowers potassium by approximately 0.5 to 1.0 mmol/L but is slower than calcium gluconate; with ECG changes, membrane stabilisation is the first step. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: IV insulin 0.1 unit/kg with 2 mL/kg 10% dextrose over 30 minutes
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose shifts potassium intracellularly but takes 15 to 30 minutes; ECG changes require immediate membrane stabilisation first. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10% 0.5 mL/kg over 5 minutes
options.rationale: Membrane-stabilising calcium gluconate is the immediate first step in hyperkalaemia with ECG changes; it acts within minutes to protect the myocardium while potassium-lowering treatments take effect. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg as the first intervention
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is reserved for acidotic hyperkalaemia and is less effective than calcium for membrane stabilisation; calcium gluconate should be first when ECG changes are present. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (resonium) 1 g/kg orally
options.rationale: Resonium acts over hours and is not appropriate first-line when ECG changes demand minutes-fast action; it may be added later but is not the immediate intervention. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperkalaemia)

explanation.summary: Hyperkalaemia management is staged: protect the myocardium (IV calcium gluconate), shift potassium intracellularly (insulin-dextrose, salbutamol, bicarbonate if acidotic), and remove potassium (resonium, dialysis). In children with CKD, dietary potassium loads from bananas, oranges and potatoes can precipitate dangerous hyperkalaemia. Pause ACE inhibitor or ARB. Calcium gluconate 10 percent 0.5 mL/kg IV over 5 minutes acts in 2 to 3 minutes and lasts 30 to 60 minutes; repeat if ECG changes persist after 5 to 10 minutes. Definitive management may require dialysis in CKD.
key_points: ECG changes = immediate IV calcium gluconate first
Insulin-dextrose, salbutamol, bicarbonate = intracellular shift
Resonium and dialysis = elimination
Pause ACE inhibitors/ARBs and review dietary load
Pseudohyperkalaemia in haemolysed sample - check repeat
explanation.pearls: Calcium gluconate 0.5 mL/kg of 10% (max 20 mL) is the only step that protects the myocardium within minutes - everything else only shifts or removes potassium.

stem: A 4-year-old previously well boy presents with 3 weeks of pallor, intermittent fever, bony aches in the lower limbs at night, and easy bruising. His mother noted petechiae on his shins this morning. He has been refusing to walk for the past 2 days. On examination he has cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and scattered bruises. He has no rash on his face. He is otherwise stable. His FBC and metabolic panel return abnormal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124, RR 26, BP 102/64, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 38.4 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Pallor, cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy 1 to 2 cm, hepatomegaly 4 cm below costal margin, splenomegaly 6 cm, scattered bruises on shins, petechiae
data_table.Investigations: Hb 64 g/L, WCC 42 x10^9/L (blasts on film), platelets 12 x10^9/L, urate 0.62 mmol/L, K 5.6, phosphate 2.4, calcium 1.95, LDH 1850 U/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 60 mg/m2/day immediately in the ED
options.rationale: Pre-treatment steroids before tissue diagnosis can obscure leukaemia subtype and trigger tumour lysis without monitoring; treatment must be initiated by oncology after diagnosis. (Source: COG/ANZCHOG)

options.text: Transfuse platelets to above 100 then discharge for outpatient haematology
options.rationale: Outpatient management of suspected acute leukaemia with tumour lysis features is unsafe; he requires urgent admission and oncology referral. (Source: COG/ANZCHOG)

options.text: Transfer to a paediatric oncology unit with IV fluids and allopurinol
options.rationale: Newly suspected acute leukaemia with high WCC and biochemical tumour lysis features (high K, urate, phosphate, low calcium) requires urgent paediatric oncology transfer, IV hyperhydration and allopurinol or rasburicase. (Source: COG/ANZCHOG tumour lysis syndrome guidelines)

options.text: Bone marrow aspirate in ED by the on-call medical registrar
options.rationale: Bone marrow aspirate is essential but performed by the paediatric oncology team to ensure adequate sample, cytogenetics, and minimal residual disease testing. (Source: COG/ANZCHOG)

options.text: Arrange CT chest and abdomen, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 24 hour review
options.rationale: Routine imaging is not the next step; the priority is oncology transfer and tumour lysis prevention. Antibiotics are only added if febrile neutropenia is suspected. (Source: COG/ANZCHOG)

explanation.summary: Paediatric acute leukaemia (most commonly B-ALL at this age) presents with marrow failure (anaemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia), bone pain, lymphadenopathy and organomegaly. Tumour lysis syndrome (high urate, high potassium, high phosphate, low calcium) may precede or follow chemotherapy and is more common in bulky disease, high WCC, T-cell ALL and Burkitt lymphoma. Initial management is IV hyperhydration (2 to 3 times maintenance), allopurinol or rasburicase, monitoring electrolytes 4-hourly, and urgent transfer to paediatric oncology. Avoid potassium-containing fluids and minimise nephrotoxins.
key_points: Triad: marrow failure, bony pain, organomegaly
Tumour lysis: high K, urate, PO4, low Ca
IV hyperhydration without potassium
Allopurinol; rasburicase if urate above 0.5 mmol/L
Urgent paediatric oncology transfer
explanation.pearls: Rasburicase converts urate to allantoin and works within hours - reserve it for WCC above 100 x10^9/L or urate above 0.5 mmol/L; check G6PD first because it causes haemolysis in G6PD deficiency.

stem: A 3-week-old infant is brought to a metropolitan ED with 5 days of progressive coughing paroxysms ending in apnoea and cyanosis, followed by a high-pitched inspiratory whoop. Her older sister (4 years) had a prolonged cough for 3 weeks but has not been tested. The mother had pertussis vaccine 4 years ago but was not boosted in this pregnancy. Pernasal pertussis PCR is positive. There is a 7-week-old cousin in the same household. The infant is admitted to the high-dependency unit on continuous monitoring.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 between paroxysms, RR 60 with paroxysms, SpO2 92% off oxygen, temperature 37.2 degrees C
data_table.Examination: Coughing paroxysms with cyanosis, post-tussive vomiting, otherwise alert
data_table.Investigations: Pertussis PCR positive, FBC: lymphocytosis 18 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antimicrobial plan?

options.text: Supportive care only; pertussis self-limits and antibiotics do not alter course
options.rationale: Although antibiotics may not alter the cough course once paroxysmal, they reduce transmission and are recommended; contact prophylaxis is also needed. (Source: CDNA Pertussis SoNG)

options.text: Amoxicillin 25 mg/kg three times daily for the infant, no contact treatment
options.rationale: Amoxicillin is not effective against Bordetella pertussis; macrolides are the first-line. (Source: CDNA Pertussis SoNG)

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for the infant, no contact prophylaxis given
options.rationale: TMP-SMX is a second-line for macrolide intolerance and is not used in infants under 2 months; contact prophylaxis is also required. (Source: CDNA Pertussis SoNG)

options.text: Azithromycin for the infant plus prophylaxis to all household contacts under 6 months
options.rationale: Macrolide treatment of the index infant reduces transmission rather than altering clinical course this late, and post-exposure prophylaxis is offered to all household contacts of infants under 6 months. (Source: CDNA Pertussis SoNG; eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Erythromycin 40 mg/kg/day for the infant in the first month of life
options.rationale: Erythromycin in infants under 1 month is associated with infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis; azithromycin is preferred in this age group. (Source: CDNA Pertussis SoNG)

explanation.summary: Pertussis (whooping cough) in infants under 6 months carries the highest morbidity and mortality, particularly under 3 months who may present with apnoea, cyanosis and pulmonary hypertension rather than the classic whoop. Australian CDNA guidance is azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 5 days as first-line treatment and post-exposure prophylaxis. Erythromycin is avoided in infants under 1 month because of pyloric stenosis association. Contact prophylaxis is offered to household members of any infant under 6 months, vulnerable adults (pregnant women in the third trimester, healthcare workers, childcare workers) and unimmunised under 1 year. Boostrix in third-trimester pregnancy is the most effective prevention.
key_points: Azithromycin first-line - 5 day course
Erythromycin avoided under 1 month (pyloric stenosis)
Post-exposure prophylaxis for household and at-risk contacts of infants under 6 months
Boostrix in 3rd trimester pregnancy is preventive
PCR + culture preferred over serology in acute illness
explanation.pearls: If a pregnant woman in the third trimester is in the household of a pertussis case and has not had a Boostrix booster in this pregnancy, she also needs azithromycin prophylaxis (and the booster as soon as practical).

subtopic_detail: Late preterm sepsis vs HSV: empirical cover
stem: A 9-day-old late-preterm female infant (born at 35 weeks, now home with mother) presents to a metropolitan ED with 12 hours of poor feeding, increased sleeping, temperature 38.1 degrees C and a single brief generalised seizure witnessed in the waiting room. Mother had genital ulcers diagnosed at 36 weeks as first-episode genital herpes; no antiviral suppression was given. There is no other relevant maternal history. Capillary glucose is 3.6 mmol/L. The neonatal team has been called. IV access is patent.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 64, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 38.1 degrees C, BP 64/36 (low for age)
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, anterior fontanelle flat, mottled extremities, post-ictal but rousable, no rash currently visible
data_table.Investigations: FBC, blood culture, CSF, urine sent. Capillary glucose 3.6 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antimicrobial cover?

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin only
options.rationale: Empirical cover for an unwell late-preterm 9-day-old must include cefotaxime (or ceftriaxone if appropriate), ampicillin for Listeria and group B Streptococcus, and aciclovir for HSV given the maternal history. (Source: RWH/RCH Neonatal sepsis; ASHM Perinatal HSV)

options.text: IV cefotaxime plus amphotericin B only
options.rationale: Routine empirical antifungal cover is not indicated; Listeria cover and HSV cover are the priorities here. (Source: RWH/RCH Neonatal sepsis)

options.text: IV flucloxacillin plus gentamicin only
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin + gentamicin does not cover Listeria or HSV; this combination is more appropriate for late-onset Staphylococcus aureus sepsis. (Source: RWH/RCH Neonatal sepsis)

options.text: IV vancomycin plus cefotaxime only
options.rationale: Vancomycin is not first-line in this scenario; ampicillin covers Listeria and group B Streptococcus more reliably, and HSV cover is missing. (Source: RWH/RCH Neonatal sepsis)

options.text: IV ampicillin plus cefotaxime plus aciclovir
options.rationale: First-episode maternal genital HSV diagnosed late in pregnancy without antiviral suppression places the neonate at very high risk of HSV disease; ampicillin + cefotaxime covers bacterial sepsis and meningitis, and aciclovir 20 mg/kg every 8 hours covers HSV. (Source: RWH/RCH Neonatal sepsis; ASHM Perinatal HSV)
